segments.s703-g161: همه is the subject/adverbial 'everyone' or 'all'. ویژه here functions adverbially ('with emphasis' / 'together') — a poetic usage rather than the ordinary adjective 'special'. گفتند is 3rd-person plural past of گفتن ('they said'); what follows is reported content. کایدون is an old/poetic noun meaning a treacherous plot or plotter used here as the idea of treachery; کنیم is 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/optative ('let us do / let us make') used to express a collective proposal or intention. Grammatically this cluster is: subject/adverbial + reporting verb + proposed action in the optative form.
segments.s703-g162: که introduces a subordinate (purpose/result) clause here meaning 'so that' or 'in order that'. کوه و بیابان are coordinated nouns 'mountain and plain/desert'. پر ... از is the fixed phrase 'full (of)'; پر is the adjective 'full', از introduces the content ('of'/'with'). خون is the material 'blood' (here implying bloodshed). کنیم repeats the 1st-plural optative 'let us make/let us fill', linking the purpose clause to the proposal in the previous segment.
segments.s703-g145: دل خسرو (noun + name) is a possessive phrase ‘Khosrow’s heart’. از درد ایشان is a prepositional phrase ‘from/with the pain/grief for them’. بخست is an archaic past-tense verb (3rd person singular) meaning ‘closed’ or ‘was closed’; word order places the verb after its topical object phrase, typical in poetic narrative.
segments.s703-g146: تن خسته زندگان is a noun phrase ‘the tired bodies of the living’ (تن + adjective + plural noun). راببست is the verb with the object marker RA fused before the verb (ر + ببست) and is a past action ‘he shut/closed’ or ‘he ended’ (3rd sing.). In classical/poetic Persian the object marker can attach before the verb as here.
segments.s703-g147: همه کشتگان را به هم برفکند is a compacted word order meaning ‘they cast/threw all the slain together’. همه کشتگان را is the direct object ‘all the slain’, به هم (‘together’) modifies how they were cast, and برفکند is the past verb ‘threw down/scattered’ (3rd pl.). The poetry fuses particles (رابهم) but the meaning is ‘gathered/laid them together’.
segments.s703-g148: تلی گشت = ‘it became a heap’ (تلی + گشت ‘became’). برسان کوه بلند functions as a comparative/measure phrase ‘to the height/size of a high mountain’ — برسان here carries the sense ‘reaching/amounting to’ and کوه بلند is the standard of comparison. The clause describes the piled corpses forming a mound equal to a tall mountain.
segments.s703-g149: همی‌خواندند ایش is an iterative past construction ‘they kept calling to him’ (همی- marks repeated/continuous action; -ند is 3rd-plural). بهرام is the vocative/addressee. چید is a concise verb here meaning roughly ‘seize/take’ or ‘pluck’ in the sense of urging action; the poet uses a terse verb where a fuller command follows.
segments.s703-g150: ببرید is an imperative/jussive sense ‘take away’ or ‘carry off’ (form used to urge action). خسرو is the possessor/affected person and the small clause ز رومی امید (z + origin + noun) means ‘the hope of Khosrow from the Romans’ — i.e. ‘Khosrow’s hope in the Romans’. Word order places the object phrase after the verb, and ز marks the source/relationship (‘from/of’).
segments.s703-g151: همی‌گفت is an iterative past ‘he would say’ or ‘he kept saying’ (narrative habitual). اگر نیز رومی دو بار introduces a conditional ‘if even the Romans (were to come) twice’ — نیز here has the sense ‘even/also’ emphasizing the hypothetic, and بار means ‘occasion/time’ (دو بار = twice).
segments.s703-g152: کند همی is a combination where کند is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive ‘should do/would do’ and همی gives an iterative/emphatic flavour (‘still/indeed’); together they express a repeated or emphatic action. برین گونه is an older compounded form of بر این گونه ‘in this manner’ (ین = orthographic/old form of این). بر کارزار = ‘on the battlefield’ (بر = on/against + کارزار battlefield).
segments.s703-g129: همه رو میان دلشکسته شدند is a clause with a plural theme followed by the past copula‌شدن. همه رو (literally “all faces”) functions as the quantified subject/phrase; میان is a locative/adverbial meaning “among / in the midst,” and دلشکسته شد ند is the past plural of شدن meaning “became heart‑broken.” The verb comes at the end as in Persian syntax and the plural ending -ند agrees with the plural sense.
segments.s703-g130: به دل‌پاک بی‌جنگ خسته شدند is a second clause sharing the same past verb (شدند). به introduces the affected group (dative/for‑phrase); دل‌پاک بی‌جنگ is a noun phrase where دل‌پاک “pure‑hearted” is modified by بی‑جنگ (“without war,” an archaic/poetic compound meaning inexperienced in battle), and خسته شدند is past plural ‘they became weary.’ The compound modifier order and the negative prefix بی‑ are typical of classical Persian.
segments.s703-g131: همی‌ریخت بطریق خونین سرشک places the imperfective past verb همی‌ریخت (‘was pouring’, with the frequentative/continuous prefix همی-) before its noun. بطریق is a prepositional phrase ‘in the manner of / as,’ خونین is an adjective ‘bloody,’ and سرشک is the noun ‘teardrop / tear’; together they mean ‘tears were pouring in a bloody fashion / like blood.’
segments.s703-g132: همی رخ پر از آب و دل پر ز رشک is two parallel nominal predicates. همی (here) is an emphatic/adverbial particle ‘still / even then’; رخ پر از آب = ‘(her) face full of water’ uses پر after the noun to mean ‘full (of),’ and دل پر ز رشک = ‘(her) heart full from/of jealousy’ uses poetic ز (variant of از) to mean ‘from / with.’ The structure links two descriptive states of face and heart.
segments.s703-g133: بیامد ز گردنکشان ده هزار uses the past verb بیامد (‘came’). In classical Persian the verb can appear before the numeral phrase; ز گردنکشان ‘from the neck‑slashers’ means ‘from the rebels’ (an epithet), and ده هزار is the numeral ‘ten thousand’ indicating how many came. Word order is poetic/inverted but the relation is ‘ten thousand came from the rebels.’
segments.s703-g134: همه جاثلیقان گرد و سوار is a nominative noun phrase that continues/specifies the arriving force. همه ‘all’ quantifies جاثلیقان (the plural of جاثلیق, ‘Catholicos’ / senior ecclesiastical figure). گرد و سوار is a paired expression meaning ‘foot and horse / footmen and horsemen’ (a classical poetic pairing), and the whole phrase is understood as part of the group that arrived (the verb is supplied from the previous clause).
segments.s703-g135: یکی حمله بردند زان سان که کوه opens with یکی (‘one / once / a certain’) introducing the action; حمله بردند is the idiom ‘they launched an attack’ (بردن used with حمله). زان سان که is a fixed sequence meaning ‘in such a way that / so that,’ and کوه begins the result clause ‘the mountain…’ — the sentence thus sets up a cause/result: an attack so violent that…
segments.s703-g136: بدرید ز آواز رومی گروه is the result clause: بدرید (‘tore / was split’) is a past verb describing a violent rupture; ز آواز means ‘from/by the sound/cry,’ and رومی گروه is a noun phrase ‘the Roman/Byzantine host’ with رومی as attributive adjective. Together: ‘the mountain was split by the cry/sound of the Roman troop.’ Note the poetic use of ز = از and the compact word order.
segments.s703-g17: This is a compact clause: یلان (subject, plural) + سینه را (direct object marked by را) + گفت (past tense ‘said’) + برقلبگاه (directional phrase 'to the heart/seat of the heart'). Note that را marks سینه as what is being addressed; برقلبگاه is a compound prepositional/adverbial phrase (بـ + قلبگاه) giving the addressee-place of the speech.
segments.s703-g18: همی‌باش is an imperative/form of command here (‘be, remain’); همی gives a continuous/iterative/poetic flavour. تا functions as a temporal conjunction 'until'. پیش روی is a compound meaning 'in front of/in the presence of' (پیش = before; روی = face), and سپاه is the object 'the army/host'. The clause as a whole records the content of the command.
segments.s703-g19: که begins a causal/explanatory clause here ('for' / 'that'), followed by از لشکر امروز which locates the speaker 'from the army of today' (از = from; لشکر = host; امروز = today). جنگی منم places the predicate 'I am a fighter' (جنگی used as a substantive 'fighter/warrior', منم = 'I am'). Word order is topical: the locative/partitive phrase comes before the copular predicate.
segments.s703-g20: بگاه here is a temporal preposition/phrase 'at the time of'. گریزش contains the clitic -ش (third person) giving 'his/its flight' or idiomatically 'the act of fleeing'; in context it means 'when fleeing occurs'. درنگی is the noun 'a pause/hesitation' and منم ('I am') yields the meaning 'I am not one who hesitates' (the negative is implied from context); overall this clause says the speaker will not delay when flight takes place.
segments.s703-g21: نگه کرد is a verbal phrase 'cast a look/gazed' with نگه (gaze) + کرد (past auxiliary/do). خسرو is the subject (proper name). بدان is a fused prepositional/demonstrative (ب = 'to/at' + دان functioning as 'that' here) so بدان رزمگاه = 'at that battlefield'. The clause literal order is Subject + Verb + Prepositional phrase (poetic flexibility).
segments.s703-g22: جهان دید is 'he saw the world' (جهان = world/scene; دید = saw). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying the following phrase. زلشکر here is a poetic contraction (ز + لشکر) meaning 'of/from the host', and سیاه qualifies that host as 'black/dark'. The phrase thus means 'he saw the whole scene — a black host everywhere.'
segments.s703-g23: This is a stacked string of epithets modifying رخ (face): رخ (face) + شید (beloved/envied figure) + تابان (shining) + چوکام (chū-kām, 'as desired' / 'to his will') + هژبر (a proper name). In Persian poetic order modifiers often follow the head noun; here the sequence builds a descriptive label for the person (Hojabr).
segments.s703-g24: همی here marks continuous or iterative action ('kept/was raining'); تیغ بارید is a pictorial clause 'swords rained' (تیغ = sword, بارید = rained/fell). گفتی is an archaic/simile particle here meaning 'as if' or 'one would say' (not the straightforward past 'you said'), followed by ز ابر 'from the cloud' (ز = from; ابر = cloud). Together: 'swords rained down, as if from a cloud.'
segments.s703-g25: A coordinated list of proper names/titles joined by the conjunction و (and). Word order places the coordinated noun phrases in sequence; وشاه is «و + شاه» (= “and the Shah”). This whole noun phrase is the subject of the following verb.
segments.s703-g26: ببالا is an adverb meaning 'upward/on high' modifying the past plural verb گذشتند (they passed/ascended). زان is a prepositional/relational particle here meaning 'from that', and رزمگاه names the place 'battlefield'. The sequence is adverb + verb + source phrase ('from that battlefield').
segments.s703-g27: نشستند is a past plural verb 'they sat' (verb precedes the subject here, a common poetic order). بر + کوه is a locative phrase 'on the mountain'; دوک appears as a place-name or modifier of کوه (uncertain here), and آن سران ('those chiefs/heads') is the subject placed after the locative phrase.
segments.s703-g28: نهاده is a perfect/pret. participial form 'having placed' or 'having set'; دو دیده literally 'two eyes' = 'both eyes' (object of نهاده). بفرمانبران here indicates the target or orientation (literally built on the root for 'obey/command'), so the full phrase means 'having fixed both eyes on the commanders' (i.e. looking toward the commanders).
segments.s703-g29: ازان کوه is a source/location phrase 'from that mountain'; لشکر is the object 'army/host'; همی‌دید is an archaic continuous/past form meaning 'kept seeing' or 'beheld' (همی- marking continuous aspect), and شاه is the subject placed after the verb. Literal order: 'From that mountain the army he kept seeing, the king.'
segments.s703-g30: A coordinated enumeration of parts of the army: چپ و راست 'left and right', و قلب 'and the center/core', و جناح سپاه 'and the wings of the host'. Each noun is joined by و (and) to list the army's divisions; قلب here functions as 'center' (core of the formation).
segments.s703-g31: چوبرخاست is a compound/poetic verb meaning 'suddenly rose' or 'arose'; آواز کوس is the noun phrase 'the sound/call of the war-drum' (آواز governed by کوس). از دو روی means 'from two sides' (روی here = 'side'), so the clause reads 'the sound of the drum rose from both sides.'
segments.s703-g32: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/advanced'. مردان is the subject 'men/warriors'. پر is an adjective/particle 'full (of)' and heads the adjective phrase modifying مردان; خاشجوی is an epithet (obscure/poetic) describing their disposition — together 'men full of خاشجوی' = 'men eager for fight'.
segments.s703-g33: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said' (تو + گفتی). زمین کوه آهن شدست is a predicative nominal clause: زمین (subject) + کوهِ آهن (predicate phrase 'a mountain of iron') + شدست (archaic/poetic 3rd-person perfect/copula 'has become'). Word order places the subject before the predicate phrase, then the copula.
segments.s703-g34: سپهر ... شدست is another clause with سپهر 'the sky' as subject and شدست 'has become' as copula. از بر خاک دشمن forms a prepositional/relational phrase: از (from/than/of) + بر (here 'over, upon') + خاک دشمن 'the enemy's dust/earth' — taken together indicating the sky is made/covered by (or with) the enemy's dust. The phrase order is preposition(s) + noun, then the copula.
segments.s703-g35: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. خسرو is the subject; بران گونه 'in that manner / of that kind' modifies پیکار 'battle' (بران گونه + پیکار = 'a battle of that sort'); دید is simple past 3rd-person 'he saw'. The clause follows a typical classical Persian S (subject) + adjunct + object + verb order.
segments.s703-g36: This is two coordinated small clauses with repeated دید 'he saw': فلک تار دید = 'he saw the sky TAR' where تار is an adjectival/predicative element attached after فلک; و links the clauses; زمین قار دید = 'he saw the earth (produce) a qar' where قار is an onomatopoeic/nominal element indicating a harsh cry or sound. The repeated verb frames each perception.
segments.s703-g37: به یزدان 'to God' is the indirect object introduced by به; همی‌گفت is the iterative/imperfective past 'kept saying' or 'would say'; برپهلوی is an adverbial 'beside'/'at the side (of someone)', indicating the speaker's physical/figurative position while speaking. The clause is an action directed toward God.
segments.s703-g38: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. از برتران here marks the comparison/class (literally 'from/of the superior ones' → 'than the best of them'); پاک و برتر are predicative adjectives 'pure and superior'; توی is a locative/identifying particle here functioning like 'you are among/in (that class)' or simply addressing 'you' as the one compared. The whole gives a comparative sense ('that you are purer and more excellent than the noble/upper ones').
segments.s703-g39: که introduces a subordinate clause expressing hope/wish/expectation. برگردد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form 'may return' or 'will return' used after که to express desired/expected outcome; امروز 'today' and از رزم 'from the battle' are temporal and origin phrases; شاد is predicate adjective 'joyful'.
segments.s703-g40: که داند چنین is a rhetorical/relative construction 'who knows such a thing'; که here = 'who/that' and داند is present 'knows'. جز تو 'except you' excludes all others; ای پاک is a vocative 'O pure one' addressing the addressee; وراد in context appears as a proper name or epithet addressed vocatively. The sequence is a brief question or appeal: 'who knows this except you, O pure Varad?'
segments.s703-g137: چکاچک is an onomatopoeic noun describing the clinking/clashing of weapons; برخاست is a past verb 'arose/rose' and follows the noun (typical S V order in poetry). و simply links the next phrase. بانگ سران is a construct where بانگ (shout/cry) is modified by سران (the chiefs); word order is noun + modifier (common in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s703-g138: همان here is a demonstrative ('that/that very') pointing back to the noises and effects just described. زخم is a noun 'wound' and is followed by two instruments شمشیر and گرز that specify the causes; گران is an adjective 'heavy' qualifying گرز (heavy mace). The phrase is elliptical (no finite verb): the wounds (were) from sword and heavy mace.
segments.s703-g139: توگفتی is the past 'you said'. که introduces a content clause ('that'). دریا is the subject of the clause and بجوشد is the present/subjunctive form of جوشیدن used here poetically to mean 'would boil/seethe'; همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle signalling ongoing or prospective action.
segments.s703-g140: سپهر (the sky/heavens) is the subject; روان is an adjectival/participial modifier meaning 'moving/flowing' attached to سپهر. بر + خروشد functions as a compound verbal idea 'burst into roar' (بر used as a verbal prefix + خروشد, 'to roar'); همی again adds an archaic continuous/emphatic sense.
segments.s703-g141: ز بس is a poetic expression ('from so many' / 'because of so many'); پسِ بس often conveys magnitude — 'so many'. کشته here functions as 'the slain' (noun/participle). اندر میان سپاه is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the army' (اندر میان = among/within).
segments.s703-g142: بماندند is the past plural 'they remained / were left'. بر جای is an idiom meaning 'in place' or 'at their posts'. بربسته modifies راه (literally 'road-closed'); the adjective follows the noun it qualifies here because of poetic inversion: 'they remained in place, the road being closed.'
segments.s703-g143: ازان ('of those') marks the group being spoken about (those Romans). رومیان is the noun 'the Romans/Byzantines'. کشته شد is a passive construction ('was killed'); لشکری here names the affected group as 'a host/army' (lending the sense 'a whole host was slain'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: agent/group marker → noun → verb.
segments.s703-g144: هرآنکس که بود is a relative clause 'whoever/each one who was...' with بود as the past copula. از دلیران is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the brave' identifying membership, and سری (here) means 'a head/chief' or 'one of leading rank' — together: 'everyone who was a leader among the brave.' The clause completes the thought about who among them was slain.
segments.s703-g49: به خسرو (prepositional phrase) precedes the verb, so this opens with the addressee: 'to Khosrow'. چنین گفت is a past tense report 'said thus'. کای is an archaic vocative particle (O!), and سرفراز is an epithet 'mighty/victorious' addressing Khosrow directly.
segments.s703-g50: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (a noun/verb-root 'gaze, watch') + کن (do!) = 'look/watch!'. بدَان here functions as an independent imperative 'know/mark that' (bi- prefix + دان 'know') and directs attention to the following noun phrase. بنده دیوساز is the noun phrase being pointed out: 'the servant (bendeh) who is a diov‑sāz' (demon‑maker/ sorcerer) — a descriptive epithet.
segments.s703-g51: که introduces a subordinate clause ('for/when'). بااو = با + او 'with him'. برزم is a poetic/archaic locative/adverbial referring to the field/battle (here 'in the fight'), and اندر is the old preposition 'in/within'. آویختی is a past form meaning 'you clung/engaged' (here 'you grappled/locked in combat').
segments.s703-g52: چو او = 'when he' (چو as temporal conjunction + او). کامران is used as an adjective 'fortunate/successful' (not a proper name), شد is the past/copular 'became', then تو (you) is the subject of بگریختی 'you fled' (past). The sequence describes the contrast: as he prospered, you ran away.
segments.s703-g53: ببین is an imperative 'see/look'. از چپ لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the army's left' (i.e. the left wing). و دست راست links 'and the right hand/wing' — two directions to be scanned. Word order places the direction phrases after the verb in the usual Persian pattern.
segments.s703-g54: که تا here introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that' (که + تا = 'in order that/so that'). از میان = 'from among', دلیران 'the brave (men)', and کجاست is the fused question/relative 'where is (he)' (کجا + ست). The whole clause specifies the aim of looking: to find where he stands among the heroes.
segments.s703-g55: کنون 'now' sets the time. تا expresses purpose 'so that / in order that'. بیاموزمش is first‑person verb + object‑clitic ('I teach him' with the bi‑ prefix forming the present/subjunctive sense used for purpose: 'that I may teach him'). کارزار is the object 'warfare / battle (art of fighting)'.
segments.s703-g56: ببیند is third‑person subjunctive/jussive with the be‑ prefix 'let/may he see'. دل و رزم مردان کار is a genitive chain: 'the hearts (courage) and the battle‑deeds (کار) of the men' — i.e. their spirits and martial actions; کار here means 'deed/affair'. The clause names what he should see in order to be taught.
segments.s703-g81: نهاده is a perfective/past participle ('placed, put, fixed') used here in a circumstantial clause ('having fixed'). بکوت is the poetic contraction of به سکوت ('into silence'); the sequence نهاده بکوت ... چشم therefore means 'having fallen/been put into silence and (having) [his] eye toward Bahram'. The preposition به before بهرام marks direction/target, and چشم (gaze/eye) follows as the thing 'placed' — a syntactic inversion common in epic style.
segments.s703-g82: دو دیده means 'two eyes' (i.e. both eyes); پر از is the standard 'full of' construction (پر + از + N). دل پر ز uses ز (poetic variant of از) with پر in the same 'full of' pattern: 'heart full of anger'. Word order places the quantity/adjective before the noun (دو دیده) and repeats پر for both eyes and heart.
segments.s703-g83: چو here = 'when'; رومی is the subject ('a Rûmî/Byzantine'). به نیزه درآمد is an idiomatic past action 'came up/advanced with a spear' (درآمد is past 3rd-person), and زجای is the fused prepositional phrase 'from the place' (ز + جای). Note the compact, poetic linking of motion (به نیزه درآمد) with origin (زجای).
segments.s703-g84: جهانجوی is the agent here ('the world-seeker', i.e. an ambitious fighter). بر جای = 'on/upon the spot' and بفشارد پای is a classical narrative verb form: بفشارد (prefix ب- + verb stem + ـد) functions as a 3rd-person past/punctual action in Ferdowsi's style, meaning 'pressed'; پای is the object 'foot'. The clause describes a decisive planting/pressing of the foot on the place.
segments.s703-g85: چو = 'when'; نیزه نیامد literally 'the spear did not come' (نیامد is past negative 'did not come' — here meaning the spear failed or was unavailable/useful). برو کارگر is a terse descriptive phrase: کارگر is used adjectivally as 'effective/valiant', and برو here functions as an attributive adjective in archaic/poetic usage ('brave/forward'), so the phrase refers to the capable fighter who steps in. (The short poetic ordering leaves the attributive przed noun without extra particles.)
segments.s703-g86: بروی is a form of the verb 'to go/advance' (here 'he went/advanced'); اندر آورد is a compound meaning 'brought in' or 'produced' (اندر = in/into, آورد = brought); جنگی is the adjective 'military/war-' modifying سپر 'shield'. The clause therefore narrates that the man advanced inward/forward and brought out a war-shield.
segments.s703-g87: یکی is the indefinite 'one'/'someone' (a certain person). تیغ زد is the verbal phrase 'struck with a sword' (تیغ = sword; زد = struck, past 3rd-person of زدن). بر سر و گردنش is the prepositional attachment 'upon his head and neck' — note the enclitic possessive ش attached to گردن marking 'his'.
segments.s703-g88: که introduces the result/explanatory clause 'so that/and'. تاسینه = تا + سینه = 'as far as the chest' (up to the chest). ببرید is the past tense 'he cut/cut off' (here from بریدن) — the supplied lemma list does not show this verbal sense, so it is marked -1; the remainder تیره تنش = 'his dark body' (تیره 'dark/black' adjectival, تن 'body', ش = his).
segments.s703-g153: The phrase is an imperative instruction addressed to 'you': 'جهان را' (the world) is fronted and marked with the object/topic marker را to show what is to be judged; 'تو' is the vocative/subject pronoun ('thou/you') and 'دان' is the archaic/poetic imperative 'consider/think of'. 'بی‌لشکر روم' is a noun phrase 'Rome without an army' functioning as the predicative complement (what the world should be considered as).
segments.s703-g154: 'همان' ('that very') points to the sword; 'تیغ پولاد را' ('the steel sword') is object/topic-marked with را, and final 'موم دان' is again the imperative 'consider (it) wax' (موم = wax). Word order places the predicate ('موم') before the imperative verb 'دان', a common poetic inversion: literally 'that steel sword, consider (it) wax.'
segments.s703-g155: 'به سرگس' is a prepositional phrase 'to the dishevelled (one)' (سرگس used adjectivally here); 'چنین گفت' = 'said thus' (چنین before the verb gives the manner), and 'پس شهریار' places the adverb 'پس' ('then') before the title 'شهریار' (king). The verb 'گفت' appears before the subject ('شهریار'), a normal poetic inversion where verb precedes the subject.
segments.s703-g156: The conjunction 'که' introduces the reported imperative clause: 'فردا' = 'tomorrow' (time adverb), 'مبر' is the negative imperative 'do not take/send' (archaic form), 'جنگیان را' = 'the warriors' marked with را as definite object, and 'به کار' = 'into action/to the task' (prepositional phrase). Together: 'that tomorrow do not send the warriors into action.'
segments.s703-g157: 'تو فردا بیاسای' is a direct imperative: 'تو' (vocative 'you'), 'فردا' (tomorrow), 'بیاسای' = 'rest/be at ease' (imperative). 'تا' here is the subordinating conjunction 'until/so that' introducing the clause giving the condition/purpose for the rest, and 'من سپاه' begins that subordinate clause ('I' as subject, 'سپاه' = 'an army' as its object).
segments.s703-g158: 'بیارم' is a first-person verbal form meaning 'I will bring / I bring' (classical present/subjunctive used for a future/intent), 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from', 'ایرانیان' = 'the Iranians' (source of the troops), and 'کینه خواه' is a noun+participle compound: 'کینه' = 'hatred/grudge' + 'خواه' = 'desiring/seeking', i.e. 'those who seek vengeance' modifying 'ایرانیان'.
segments.s703-g159: 'بایرانیان' functions as the agent/subject phrase (here referring to the Iranians or 'with the Iranians'), followed by the verb 'گفت' ('said'); 'فردا' is the time adverb 'tomorrow', and 'به جنگ' is the prepositional phrase 'to/into war'. The time and direction phrase set up the content of what was said.
segments.s703-g160: 'شما را' marks 'you' as the affected party with the object marker را; 'بباید' is the modal 'must/ought' (classical form), and 'شدن' ('to become/to be') follows to create a passive/necessitative sense ('you must be made/sent'); 'بی‌درنگ' is an adverb 'immediately' that modifies the whole clause. The phrase conveys obligation that 'you must be [sent] at once.'
segments.s703-g73: Subject + object + verbal phrase: یلان (subject) precedes the object سینه بهرام را (noun + possessive + object marker را) and the light-verb بانگ کرد follows. را is the Persian definite/object marker attached to سینه بهرام. بانگ کرد is a compound where بانگ (a shout) is the noun and کرد (past of کردن) makes it into 'made a cry/ shouted.'
segments.s703-g74: که introduces the content of the shout (complement clause) here: 'that...' or more naturally quoted speech. بیدارباش‌ای is an imperative interjection 'Wake up!' addressed to سوار 'rider/horseman'; نبرد ('battle') is used as a descriptive epithet modifying سوار ("rider of battle").
segments.s703-g75: چو here is temporal 'when'; آمد is simple past 'came.' یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (= someone/a certain one) introducing دیو 'demon/monster.' چون ... پیل مست is a simile: 'like an elephant, drunk' (پیل 'elephant' followed by the adjective مست 'drunk' — adjective follows the noun in this poetic order).
segments.s703-g76: A compact instrumental/descriptive phrase: کمندی (a lariat/snare) is presented first, then بفتراک (an adverbial/poetic particle here accompanying the action), with و coordinating نیزه (spear). بدست is a stative/instrumental phrase 'in (his) hand' modifying نیزه. The whole cluster describes the demon's weapons/pose.
segments.s703-g77: چو is temporal 'when' again; the clause places بهرام as subject and بشنید (past) as verb 'he heard.' تیغ از نیام is an object phrase 'the sword from the scabbard' — in idiomatic English this means 'the sword being drawn from its scabbard' (the sound or act of drawing). Word order is typical Persian: subject + verb with the object phrase preceding the verb.
segments.s703-g78: برآهخت is a past third-person-singular (poetic/archaic) form meaning 'he raised' or 'it was raised.' چون باد is a simile 'like the wind' describing the quick motion. و links the next action: برگفت here means 'he spoke/uttered' and نام is the direct object 'a name' (i.e., he cried out a name).
segments.s703-g79: چوخسرو is a fused temporal phrase (چو + خسرو) 'when Khosrow...' followed by چنان 'thus/so' and دید 'saw' (past). برپای خاست is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'he rose to his feet' — برپای functions adverbially 'on foot' and خاست is past 'arose/stood up.'
segments.s703-g80: ازان is 'from that' (از + آن) pointing back to a place; کوه‌سر is 'mountain-summit/crest.' سر here is the anatomical 'head' (object of برآورد), and برآورد is a past/formal poetic verb 'raised/lifted up' (here used transitively). راست postmodifies the clause as an adverb 'straight/directly.' The sequence describes him lifting his head from the mountain-crest in a straight/direct posture.
segments.s703-g97: چو is a subordinating particle meaning 'when/if' here (conditional/time). کوت هزاره is a compact noun phrase: کوت (a small fort/encampment or similar archaic/poetic noun) followed by هزاره ('thousand'), so together 'the (kūt) of a thousand' or 'thousand kôts'; به ایران و روم is a directional/locative phrase ('to/into Iran and Rome') attached to that noun phrase and waiting for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s703-g98: نبینند is a negative present (or jussive/subjunctive) third-person plural form 'they do not/they will not see' (poetic word order places the verb before the adverb). هرگز is the negative adverb 'never'. به آباد بوم is the object/goal phrase 'to a prosperous/inhabited homeland' (آباد بوم = 'prosperous land, dwelling'), with به marking direction/association in this poetic context.
segments.s703-g99: بخندی is a second‑person present/jussive form 'you laugh' (poetic ending -ی); کنون is 'now'. زانک is a causal particle 'because/since'. اوکشته شد is a compound of the participle اوکشته ('slain') plus شد (the auxiliary 'became/was') yielding the passive/result 'he was slain'. The clause as a whole gives the reason for the addressee's laughter.
segments.s703-g100: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative expression 'consider it so / think thus' (دان used in the sense 'consider/know'). که introduces the content clause ('that'). بخت تو برگشته شد is a perfect/resultative expression: برگشته (returned) + شد (auxiliary) = 'your fortune has been turned back / has returned', with تو marking the possessor 'your'.
segments.s703-g101: بدو گفت is the prepositional phrase + verb 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it', گفت = 'said'—here the verbal sense is the past tense reported speech). خسرو من ('my Khosrow') is a vocative or address inside the reported speech. از کشتنش uses از with the verbal noun کشتن + possessive -ش to create 'concerning/for his killing' or 'because of his being killed' and functions as the object/reason in the following clause.
segments.s703-g102: نخندم is first‑person singular negative present/subjunctive 'I do not/will not laugh' (poetic -م ending). همی is an intensifier/old‑style particle ('indeed/still/it is so'). وز here functions as an archaic preposition akin to 'از' meaning 'on account of/for' linking to بریده تنش ('his severed body'): بریده (cut off/severed) + تن (body) + ش (his) = 'his severed body'.
segments.s703-g103: چنان دان که — again the formula 'consider it so/know that'. هرکس که دارد فسوس is a relative clause 'whoever has/harbors فسوس'; دارد is the present third‑person singular of داشتن 'to have/possess' (used for holding feelings/intentions), and فسوس is an old/poetic word for 'insidious whisper/evil whispering' or idle, deceitful talk — here meaning malicious inclination or treacherous whisperings.
segments.s703-g104: همو yابد is emphatic: همو ('that very one') + یابد (present tense 'finds/obtains' or 'meets with') — together 'that very person will find/receive'. از introduces the source 'from', and چرخ گردنده کوس is a poetic image: چرخ گردنده 'the turning/rotating wheel' (i.e. wheel of fortune) + کوس (literally 'war‑drum' or signal, here part of the metaphor). The whole phrase means he will suffer/receive from the turning wheel of fate (i.e. fate will strike him).
segments.s703-g41: کرابخت is the noun 'the ill‑fated one' (subject). خواهد شدن is a periphrastic future/passive construction: خواهد (future auxiliary) + شدن (infinitive/become) giving 'will become / will be made'. کندرو is the predicative noun 'frankincense' that completes شدن; the poetic order places the predicate after the auxiliary rather than the more prosaic object‑before‑verb order.
segments.s703-g42: سر نیزه is a compound meaning 'spear‑head' (سر = top/head + نیزه = spear). که introduces a relative clause 'which', and شود is the present/future subjunctive/inflected verb 'become' here giving 'that will become'. خار و خو are two coordinated nouns joined by و ('and'); خار = 'thorn' and خو here is a poetic noun (literally 'sleep' / 'slumber') forming a terse, image‑laden predicate of the relative clause.
segments.s703-g43: دل و جان (literally 'heart and life/soul') form a coordinated subject phrase referring to the inner state. خسرو names the possessor (Khosrow). پراندیشه is a compound adjective 'full of thought / pensive' describing that subject, and بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'Khosrow's heart and soul were pensive.'
segments.s703-g44: جهان پیش چشمش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'the world before his eyes' (پیش = before/in front of, چشمش = his eye). یکی بیشه بود is a simile/predicative clause: یکی 'one' + بیشه 'grove' + بود 'was' — 'was a single grove' (i.e. the whole world seemed like one grove).
segments.s703-g45: که is the relative 'which/that' linking to the previous clause. بگسست is a past tense verb 'broke / was torn apart'. کوت is the object 'cloak' (a short cloak). ازمیان سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the midst of the army/host' (از میان = from among; سپاه = host/army); altogether 'which tore the cloak from the midst of the army.'
segments.s703-g46: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. آهن 'iron' is used metonymically (weapons/metal). بکردار is an older/poetic adjective phrase attached to what follows (here conveying the notion 'of such a make/appearance' or 'in the manner/form of'); کوهی سیاه is a nominal phrase 'a black mountain' (کوهی = mountainous/of a mountain, سیاه = black). The line compresses the image: out of (or like) iron a black mountain appeared/was formed.
segments.s703-g47: بیامد is the past tense verb 'came'. دمان here (poetic/archaic) means 'foothill/the skirt (of the mountain)' and functions as a locative complement. تامیان modifies گروه: تامیان = 'the whole/entire' and گروه = 'group/host' — so 'the entire host came to the foothill.' The word order is compressed for poetic effect.
segments.s703-g48: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. نزدیک ترشد is a comparative past construction ('نزدیک‑تر شد' = 'became nearer' / 'drew nearer'). بران = 'to that / thither' (referring back to the mountain), and برز کوه = 'high mountain' (برز functioning as 'high/exalted' modifying کوه). Together: 'When they drew nearer to that high mountain.'
segments.s703-g1: چوخورشید is the fused archaic construction چو + خورشید = 'when the sun...'. برزد is an archaic past verb meaning 'came/rose' (here used intransitively). سراز (سَر + از) means 'up from / out of the head of', so سراز تیره کوه = 'up from the dark mountain's summit'; word order places the time/setting clause before the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s703-g2: خروشی is the noun 'a roar/clamor'. برآمد is a past/aperfect verb 'arose/issued forth'. زهر دو گروه here functions idiomatically as 'from both groups/hosts' (an archaic contraction; grammatically a source phrase), so زهر is not used in its literal 'poison' sense but as an old fused prepositional element. گروه = 'troop/host'.
segments.s703-g3: که گفتی introduces reported speech or 'as you said' (که = 'that/as'). گفتی here functions as the formulaic 'you said' (a verbal form in older diction). زمین is the subject 'the earth'; گشت is the past verb 'became/turned'; گردان modifies سپهر as 'revolving' (i.e. 'the revolving sky'). The clause is a reportive statement leading into the conditional in the next segment.
segments.s703-g4: گر is the conditional 'if'. از تیغها is the ablative/cause 'because of / from the swords'. تیره شد is a two-word verb phrase: تیره (dark) + شد 'became/was made', so 'became dark'. روی مهر = 'the face of Mehr' (مهر here is a divine/solar name = the sun); the clause expresses the condition under which the preceding picture would obtain.
segments.s703-g5: بیاراسته is an adjective/adverbial phrase 'unadorned / stripped / disarrayed' placed before the following phrase. میمن و میسره names the two sides (right and left); here it marks both flanks being affected. The construction gives an image of both sides being thrown into disorder.
segments.s703-g6: زمین کوه appears as two coordinated nouns (earth and mountain) with the conjunction omitted, a common poetic compression. گشت is the past 'became/turned', and آهنین یکسره is the predicate phrase 'entirely iron/steely' (آهنین = 'iron/steely', یکسره = 'completely'). The verb applies to both nouns.
segments.s703-g7: از introduces a source: 'from the sound of...'. آواز اسپان = 'the sounds/neighing of the horses' (آواز here = 'sound/cry', اسپان = 'horses/cavalry'). و بانگ سپاه = 'and the shout/cry of the army' (بانگ = 'cry/shout', سپاه = 'host/army'). The whole phrase functions as an ablative cause for the motion in the next segment.
segments.s703-g8: بیابان is the subject 'the open plain / wilderness'. همی‌جست is an archaic iterative/imperfective past 'kept leaping / surged' (همی- prefix marks continuous/aspectual action). بر + کوه راه is a locative/directional phrase 'upon the mountain road' (بر = 'onto/upon', کوه راه = 'mountain road/path').
segments.s703-g65: چو + بیند is a classical/poetic temporal clause meaning 'when (he) saw'; the phrase places the action in time. تو را is the accusative marked object ('you' with the object marker را). پیشت آید is a slightly inverted order: پیشت (before/forward of you) + آید (comes) — here a present/past-tense narrative sense 'comes/appears' describing the approach into battle. به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the battle' specifying the scene.
segments.s703-g66: تومگریز is an imperative/negative-address to the addressee 'do not flee' (classical second-person negative). تا introduces a limit 'until'. لب is literal 'lip' here used physically/poetically. نخایی is uncertain/obscure in this form; syntactically it follows تا + noun and functions as part of the limiting clause (meaning is contextually something like 'reaches/opens' — the precise lexical sense is unclear). زننگ is the sound noun 'ringing' (the clash/clang) and completes the clause 'until the ringing reaches your lips.'
segments.s703-g67: چوبشنید is a classical circumstantial temporal phrase 'on hearing' or 'when he heard.' کوت here functions syntactically as the subject (though the lexical identification is uncertain — the line treats it as the entity reacting). این سخن is the direct object 'these words' and بازگشت is the past verb 'turned back/returned.' The word order (circumstantial phrase → subject → object → verb) is typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s703-g68: چنان شد که introduces a result or consequence 'so/so it happened that...'. با باد is the instrumental/agent phrase 'with/by the wind.' انباز is an archaic/uncertain lexical item used adverbially or adjectivally here; it modifies the verb گشت, which is the past 'turned/was turned' — together giving a simile or metaphor 'was turned away / swept away as/by the wind.'
segments.s703-g69: همی‌رفت is the classical imperfect/progressive past 'he was going / he went on' — the prefix همی- marks continuous or iterative aspect in older Persian. جوشان is a participial/adjectival modifier 'seething/boiling' describing his state. ونیزه is the conjunctive + noun (و نیزه = 'and (with) spear'); بدست literally 'in hand' describes manner/instrument 'with spear in hand.' The order places the descriptive phrase before the motion verb that follows in the next segment.
segments.s703-g70: به آوردگه رفت is a prepositional phrase + past verb: به آوردگه ('to the place of contest/arena') + رفت ('went'). The simile begins with چون ('like/as') and completes with پیل مست ('an elephant drunk/intoxicated') — a conventional epic simile comparing his pace or bearing to that of a drunken elephant. Word order here is typical: destination phrase, main verb, then simile as a trailing comparative phrase.
segments.s703-g71: چو نزدیک شد is a temporal clause 'when he came near / when he drew close' (چو = when; نزدیک شد = 'became near' / 'came near'). خواست is the simple past 'he wanted/intended' and governs the following object marked phrase. بهرام را is the direct object marked by را (here 'Bahram' as the person toward whom the intended action is directed). The segment sets up intention or attempted action toward Bahram.
segments.s703-g72: برافراخت is a past transitive verb 'he raised/hoisted/uplifted.' زانگونه is an adverbial demonstrative 'in that way/thus' modifying the manner of action. زونام is a lexeme whose precise meaning is obscure in the provided senses (it is the object of the verb here — likely a specific weapon, standard, or piece of equipment in the poem), and را is the object marker. Syntactically this segment reports the concrete action that realizes the prior intention.
segments.s703-g121: بران is an adverbial/locative particle meaning 'upon that' or 'thereupon', placed before the noun phrase. زخم او = 'his wound' (noun + pronoun), and پراگند here is a poetic past‑participle/adjective 'scattered/sprinkled' modifying or functioning verbally with مشک 'musk' as the thing scattered. The whole segment therefore reads as an elliptical classical clause: 'upon that wound musk was scattered.'
segments.s703-g122: بفرمود is the honorific past tense 'he commanded/said'; پس is a temporal connective 'then'. تا introduces a purposive/result clause 'so that / in order that', and بکردند is the classical past plural 'they did / they made' (the object is the dressing), with خشک as the result/adjective 'dry' (i.e. 'make it dry'). The subject of بکردند is understood (the attendants).
segments.s703-g123: به کرباس means 'into/onto a coarse cloth' (به + noun of material). بر دوختش is the compound verb 'bar‑duxt' ('sewed on / sewed up') with the enclitic object -ش 'it' (referring to the dressing/wound); classical prefix ب- appears on the verb. همچنان is an adverb 'in the same manner / likewise', linking this action to the previous one.
segments.s703-g124: زره is 'armor' (the breastplate). دربر is a locative/adverbial compound meaning 'within / in the midst of / embraced by' (i.e. placed inside the armor). و is the conjunction 'and'. تنگ بسته میان is a sequence of adjectives/participles: تنگ 'tight/close', بسته 'closed/bound', میان 'in the middle' — together describing how it was secured inside the armor.
segments.s703-g125: به نزدیک here indicates direction 'to/near' (toward Caesar's side). قیصر is 'Caesar / the emperor'. فرستاد is the past 'he sent' and باز after it means 'back' (i.e. sent back). Together: 'he sent (him) back to the presence of Caesar.'
segments.s703-g126: که introduces the reported content ('saying that' / 'that'). شمشیر این بنده دیوساز is a noun phrase: 'the sword of this bende/servant — (the) diovsaz.' دیوساز is an epithet meaning 'demon‑maker' or 'sorcerer' used appositionally, so the clause reports that 'this servant's sword is diov‑making' (i.e. dangerous or magical).
segments.s703-g127: برین (classical compound = بر + این) + گونه = 'in this manner/way'. برد here denotes taking/winning (the victory) and is used with روز جنگ 'the day of battle' as its temporal object — 'he won the day's battle in this way.' همی is an emphatic/classical adverb (here reinforcing the statement).
segments.s703-g128: ازوگر is a fused conditional particle meaning 'if by him / were it that from him' (classical form of اگر از او). هزیمت 'rout/defeat' + شدم 'I became / I had been' gives 'if I were routed by him'. نیست ننگ = 'there is no shame' (negation before the noun). The clause expresses that being defeated by that man would not be disgraceful.
segments.s703-g89: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when." آواز تیغش is a possessive/genitive phrase (آواز + possessive suffix -ش on تیغ) meaning "the sound/voice of his sword." به خسرو marks the indirect object/target "to Khosrow," and رسید is the past tense, 3rd-person singular of رسیدن "to reach/arrive" — so literally "when the sound of his sword reached Khosrow."
segments.s703-g90: بخندید is the past, 3rd-person singular of خندیدن "to laugh." کان here is a conjunctive/simile particle meaning "as if" or "as though." زخم بهرام is a simple noun phrase (زخم + name بهرام) with دید (past of دیدن) completing the simile: "he laughed as if he had seen Bahram's wound." Note the inverted, compressed poetic order.
segments.s703-g91: نیاطوس is the proper name (subject). جنگی is an epithet/adjective "warlike" or appositive noun "warrior" qualifying the name. بتابید is an archaic/poetic past form of تابیدن here meaning "he turned/cast" (as of a glance), and چشم is the object "eye/glance." Word order is poetic (name + epithet + verb + object).
segments.s703-g92: ازان (از + آن) = "from that/thereupon" pointing back to the previous laughter. خنده خسرو = "Khosrow's laughter." آمد is the past of آمدن "came" used here in the idiomatic construction meaning "was brought/seized by"; بخشم is an archaic/fused form corresponding to به خشم "into wrath/anger." (The provided lemma senses for بخشم do not match this use, so that item is idiomatic here.) The clause therefore means "from that laughter, wrath came upon Khosrow" = "Khosrow became angry."
segments.s703-g93: به خسرو چنین گفت = "he said thus to Khosrow": به marks the indirect object "to Khosrow," چنین "thus/so," and گفت is the verb "said." کای is a vocative particle ("O..."), here addressing the addressee, and نامدار is an adjective/noun meaning "renowned" used as the vocative epithet. Note: the provided senses list for گفت did not include the verb sense, so that entry is used here as the speech verb.
segments.s703-g94: نه نیکو بود خنده درکارزار = literally "laughter in battle was not good." نه negates the adjective نیکو ("good/fit"); بود is the past/predicative copula "was." خنده در کارزار is the noun phrase "laughter in battle/at the battlefield." The clause states the moral judgment that laughing during combat is not proper.
segments.s703-g95: تو رانیست از روم جز کیمیا is compressed/poetic: تو is the 2nd-person subject, رانیست functions as a negative existential/predicate here ("are not / do not exist as"), از روم = "from Rome," and جز کیمیا = "except by/for alchemy" (i.e., only in name or by artifice). The sense conveys that the addressee is not truly Roman except by some contrivance (kīmiyā).
segments.s703-g96: دلت خیره بینم is a short clause: دلت (دل + 2nd‑person suffix ت) = "your heart," خیره = "stunned/astounded/rigid," and بینم is 1st‑person present/perspective "I see" (used by the speaker to report perception). The final phrase بکین نیا is elliptical/ambiguous in classical diction: بکین can be a poetic form of بکن ("do") or a form meaning "avenge/strike," and نیا may be a vocative/shortened address (to Niyātus) or the negative imperative "do not come" (نیا "do not come"); the line is therefore compact and susceptible to different readings — the exact intended force (e.g., "do this, Nia" vs. "do not come") is unclear from the forms alone.
segments.s703-g105: مرا گفت کز بنده بگریختی = literal word order places the object 'مرا' (me + object marker) before the verb 'گفت' (said), so this is 'he said to me'. The conjunction 'کز' is an archaic form of 'که' introducing reported speech: 'that you fled from the servant.' Note the second-person past 'بگریختی' (you fled).
segments.s703-g106: نبودت هنر تا نیاویختی = 'نبودت' uses the existential sense 'there was not in you / you lacked'; 'هنر' here means 'valor/skill'. The particle 'تا' functions temporally/causally ('until/for') linking the preceding clause to the negative past 'نیاویختی' (you did not fling yourself/attach yourself), so the whole means roughly 'you had no skill, for you did not throw yourself (at him)'.
segments.s703-g107: ازان بنده بگریختن نیست ننگ = the gerund/infinitive 'بگریختن' ('to flee') functions as the subject; 'ازان' ('from that' / 'of that') qualifies it, and the copula 'نیست' negates existence of shame: 'there is no shame in fleeing from that servant.' This is typical Persian nominalization of verbs.
segments.s703-g108: که زخمش بدین سان بود روز جنگ = 'که' here gives the reason ('for / because'); 'زخمش' = 'his wound'; 'بدین سان' is a fossilized adverbial phrase ('in this manner/thus') formed from ب + دین + سان; 'بود' is the past copula ('was'); and 'روز جنگ' ('the day of battle') specifies the time. Together: 'for his wound was thus on the day of battle.'
segments.s703-g109: وزان روی بهرام آواز داد = 'وزان' is an archaic contraction meaning 'and/from that' or 'thereupon'; 'روی' here functions adverbially ('on that account' / 'thereupon'); 'بهرام' is the subject; 'آواز داد' literally 'gave a voice' = 'cried out' or 'shouted'. Word order places the adverbial phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s703-g110: که‌ای نامداران فرخ نژاد = 'که‌ای' is here the vocative 'که ای' → 'O ...'; 'نامداران' is plural vocative 'renowned/illustrious ones'; 'فرخ نژاد' is a compound epithet 'fortunate-born / of fortunate lineage' placed after the noun as an appositive description. Together they form the address: 'O renowned, fortunate-born (men)!'.
segments.s703-g111: یلان سینه و رام و ایزد گسسپ = a list of names/epithets joined by 'و' (and). Here 'یلان', 'سینه' and 'رام' function as personal names or heroic names, and 'ایزد گسسپ' puts the honorific 'ایزد' ('god' / 'divine') before the name 'گسسپ' (Gosasp). The sequence is a simple nominal list (heroes called by name).
segments.s703-g112: مرین کشته را بست باید بر اسپ = 'مرین' is read here as a proper name (Merin) qualifying the slain; 'کشته را' is the definite object 'the slain' with object marker 'را'; 'بست' is the past verb 'bound'; 'باید' expresses necessity ('must/ought to') and follows the verb in this classical usage; 'بر اسپ' is the prepositional phrase 'upon the steed/horse'. The clause means 'Merin, the slain, must be bound upon a steed.'
segments.s703-g57: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when' that introduces an adverbial clause. بشنید is the simple past, 3rd-person singular 'he heard'; poetic word order places the verb before the subject خسرو. زکوت here functions as ز + کوت (a source marker 'from' + the noun) and links as the source of the following object این سخن; این سخن is the direct object 'this speech/remark.'
segments.s703-g58: دلش گشت is a predicative phrase: دل + possessive clitic ش 'his' plus گشت (past of گشتن) meaning 'became/turned.' پردرد is an adjectival compound 'full of pain / painful' describing the heart. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' کین کهن is a noun plus adjective: کین 'vengeance/hatred' qualified by کهن 'old/ancient.' The whole segment states the resulting inner change.
segments.s703-g59: کجا گفت here functions as an exclamatory/quotative phrase 'he exclaimed / he said (how...)'; گفت is the past of گفتن 'said' introducing the quoted subordinate clause that follows. کز is the enclitic conjunction 'that/from which' introducing the content of the quotation; بنده is the noun 'servant/slave' and بگریختی is 2nd-person past 'you fled' (object of کز). Together the clause expresses astonishment at someone fleeing from a slave.
segments.s703-g60: This is a compact predicate: سلیح سواران is a genitival phrase 'the weapons/armament of the horsemen' (in poetry the ezāfe may be elided), and فروریختی is the simple past 'fell down / collapsed / were shattered.' The verb agrees with the (implicit) subject and completes the complaint/assertion in the speech.
segments.s703-g61: ورا is the oblique pronoun 'to him' (here marking the person who would have received a reply). زان سخن means 'to/for that speech' ('زان' = 'from that/that'). هیچ پاسـخ نداد is a negated past clause: هیچ 'not at all' + پاسخ 'reply' + نداد 'he did not give' (past of دادن). The whole line says he gave no reply to that remark.
segments.s703-g62: دلش گشت is again 'his heart became...' with گشت = 'became.' پرخون is an adjective 'bloodied, full of blood.' The second half, سر پر ز باد, is an inverted descriptive phrase: سر 'head', پر 'full (of)' + ز (poetic 'of/from') + باد 'wind' — literally 'head full of wind,' an idiomical image (dazed/stunned). The coordination with و links the two physical/affective images.
segments.s703-g63: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said.' پس is a temporal/sequence adverb 'then,' and کوت را is the marked (را) direct object / addressee 'to Kūt' (in poetry a name or epithet can appear here); شهریار is the subject 'the king.' Word order is inverted for emphasis: 'thus said then the king to Kūt.'
segments.s703-g64: که introduces the content of the king's speech ('that / who'). روپیش is a locative/adverbial 'before/in front' placed before the demonstrative phrase; آن مرد ابلق سوار is a descriptive nominal group: آن 'that' + مرد 'man/hero' + ابلق 'piebald' (describing his mount or aspect) + سوار 'rider/horseman.' The segment names the man the king is pointing out.
segments.s703-g9: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بهرام is the subject (proper name). جنگی is an epithet/appositive meaning 'a fighter/warrior'. بـ + دان + بنگرید together function as 'looked at that' (ب as the preposition 'to/at' combining with a demonstrative, and بنگرید is the past tense 'looked'); the components are inverted in poetic word order but together form 'looked at that person/thing'.
segments.s703-g10: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one / someone'. خنجر آبگون is the noun phrase 'a bluish dagger' with the adjective following the noun (typical in Persian poetry). برکشید is a past transitive verb 'lifted / drew' (here: drew a weapon), placed after its object in the SOV order.
segments.s703-g11: نیامد is a negated past 'did not come'. به دل‌ش اندرون is the prepositional phrase 'into his heart' (به + دل + enclitic -ش = 'into his heart'), with اندرون reinforcing 'inside/within'. ترس و بیم are coordinated nouns 'fear and dread'. The verb precedes the object here (poetic inversion): literally 'did not come into his heart fear and dread'.
segments.s703-g12: دل شیر = 'the heart (courage) of a lion' (a metaphor for courage). دربیشه is an adverbial phrase 'in the grove/wood'. شد is the past 'became'. بدو is 'to him/unto it' and نیم means 'half'. The clause literally says 'the lion's heart in the grove became to him half' (poetic phrasing indicating a partial or diminished share/portion — the exact idiom is compressed in the epic style).
segments.s703-g13: به ایرانیان گفت = 'he said to the Iranians'. گفت is a reporting verb introducing reported speech or an order. صف برکشید is a reported command: صف 'line/battle formation' and برکشید here functions as an instruction 'raise/form' (the same form can be read as a past tense or as an imperative in reported speech), so together '(he) told the Iranians, "Form the ranks!"'.
segments.s703-g14: همه کشور = 'the whole country/realm' (subject). دوک لشکر کشید is a verb phrase with لشکر 'army' as the object and کشید the past verb 'drew/assembled' placed at the end (SOV); دوک appears as an inserted element modifying or emphasizing the action (its exact local sense is ambiguous in the provided lexicon). The overall meaning is 'the whole realm drew up/assembled its army.'
segments.s703-g15: همی‌گشت is an iterative/past progressive 'kept going / went about'. گرد سپه = 'around the host/army' (گرد = around, سپه = army). یک‌تنه is an adverbial 'single‑handedly / alone' modifying the action. The verb-first poetic order emphasizes the continuing act of circling the army alone.
segments.s703-g16: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. دارد is present 'has/holds' (here 'holds' in the sense of 'has the watch'). نگه is a noun 'guard/watch' (what is being held). میسر و میمنه name the two flanks (left and right); میسر in the provided lexicon is listed as 'possible/available' but here functions as the term for the left flank (a specialized or archaic usage), while میمنه is 'the right side'. The clause thus means 'who holds the watch of the left and the right.'
segments.s703-g113: فرستید is the literary past (third‑person plural) 'sent/dispatched' used here to report an action; ز + ایدر is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from here' (ز = 'from', ایدر = 'here'); به لشکر is the direction 'to the army/host' (به = 'to', لشکر = 'army'); گهش is a genitival/locative element (گه + ش) roughly 'its place/occasion' and here belongs with 'لشکر' to indicate 'to his host/place'.
segments.s703-g114: This chunk expresses purpose/result. ب + دان here form the compound بدَان ('to/so that' or 'for that') — the writer split the items but they function together to mark purpose; تابریده is a participial/adjectival past 'exhausted/worn' modifying an implicit person; ببیند is the third‑person present/subjunctive with the prefix بـ (b‑) meaning 'may/so that he may see' (purpose); شهش is شه + ش = 'his king' (possessive suffix).
segments.s703-g115: This is a noun phrase describing placement: تن 'the body' is head noun; کوت is a short cloak (an outer garment) and رازود functions as an attributive adjective 'secret/mysterious' modifying that cloak; برپشت زین is a locative postpositional phrase 'on the back of the saddle' (برپشت = 'on the back of', زین = 'saddle').
segments.s703-g116: بتنگی here is adverbial from 'بِ تنگی' meaning 'tightly/close' (i.e. in a narrow/tight manner); ببستند is the literary past (third‑person plural) 'they bound/tied' (not merely 'closed'); مردان کین is a postnominal description 'men of vengeance' (مردان = men/warriors, کین = vengeance), identifying the agents who tied it.
segments.s703-g117: دوان اسپ is a verbless or participial phrase rendered as 'rushing/galloping steed' (دوان = 'galloping', اسپ = 'steed'); با introduces accompaniment 'with' and مرد گردن‌فراز is an appositive/adjunct 'a proud (neck‑raised) man' (گردن‌فراز is a compound adjective meaning 'proud').
segments.s703-g118: همی‌شد is the habitual/imperfective past 'kept happening' or 'would be/was doing' (همی marks continued action); به لشکر marks direction 'to the host', گه functions adverbially 'at times/sometimes', خوی + ش = خویش 'his/own' (here indicating possession of the army), and باز 'back/again' gives the sense 'return' — together 'would at times return to his own host.'
segments.s703-g119: دل خسرو is a possessive noun phrase 'Khosrow's heart' (دل = heart, خسرو = the name Khosrow); ازکوت is a causal/locative phrase 'from the fort' or 'on hearing from the fort'; شد is the past copula 'became', and دردمند is the predicate adjective 'sorrowful/in pain' describing the state of the heart.
segments.s703-g120: گشادند is past (third‑person plural) 'they opened/loosed' (here 'loosened' a binding); زان = ز + آن 'from that/from it' (indicating source); کشته is the noun 'the slain (one)' (killed person); بند کمند is a compound object 'the binding/rope of the lasso' (بند = bond/fetter, کمند = lasso/snare) — together 'they loosened the rope from that slain man.'
